data.title: Mirarth Real Estate Investment (TSE:3492) High 6.13% Yield Sparks Debate On Dividend Sustainability
data.description:
MIRARTH Real Estate Investment (TSE:3492) has put fresh numbers on the table for H1 2026, with revenue of ¥5,562 million and basic EPS of ¥2,846.89, set against trailing twelve month revenue of ¥11,157 million and EPS of ¥5,805.76 as earnings over the last year grew 10.6%. Over recent halves, revenue has moved from ¥5,334 million in H1 2025 to ¥5,595 million in H2 2025 and ¥5,562 million in H1 2026, while basic EPS has shifted from ¥2,951.10 to ¥2,958.87 and then to ¥2,846.89. This frames a set of results where rich margins and a 47.4% trailing net margin keep the focus squarely on how durable this profitability profile really is.

See our full analysis for MIRARTH Real Estate Investment.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to set these results against the most widely held narratives about MIRARTH Real Estate Investment to see which views align with the numbers and which start to look more stretched.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Stay High, But Slip From 49.1% To 47.4%

  • Trailing net margin sits at 47.4%, compared with 49.1% a year earlier, alongside trailing net income of ¥5,288 million on ¥11,157 million of revenue.
  • What stands out for a bullish view that focuses on earnings quality is that:
    • Trailing earnings grew 10.6% over the last year, and five year annualized earnings growth is reported at 12.5%. This lines up with the idea of a business that has been able to grow profits over time even as margin has eased slightly.
    • The high margin level and description of high quality earnings sit next to that modest margin slip. Anyone leaning bullish needs to weigh strong profitability against the fact that the margin is not moving higher in the latest data.

P/E Of 15.2x Versus 23.5x Peer Average

  • The units trade on a trailing P/E of 15.2x, compared with 16.3x for the wider Asian REITs group, 19.9x for a broader REITs peer set and 23.5x for the closest peer average.
  • Critics highlight a bearish angle that compares price with cash flows and fair value, and the current numbers give that argument some support:
    • The current unit price of ¥88,100 sits well above the stated DCF fair value of ¥15,764.91. Purely on that model the units are priced at several times the DCF estimate even though the P/E screens lower than peers.
    • When the DCF output points to a lower value while multiples look cheaper than the peer average, it creates tension that bearish investors can point to as a reason to question how much comfort a single ratio such as P/E really provides.
On a valuation story like this, it helps to see how others are framing the upside and the risks around MIRARTH before making any big calls, so it is worth checking how different investors are reading the same set of numbers through their own lenses.📊 Read the what the Community is saying about MIRARTH Real Estate Investment.

Cash Flow Coverage And A 6.13% Yield

  • The units offer a 6.13% dividend yield, while the latest analysis flags that this payout is not well covered by either earnings or free cash flow, and that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow.
  • What is surprising when setting this against a more cautious, bearish style narrative is how the income attractions and coverage gaps sit side by side:
    • The combination of a high yield and trailing net income of ¥5,288 million suggests solid profit in accounting terms, yet the weak coverage of both dividends and debt from cash flows means cash generation is not matching that profit profile.
    • For investors who focus on distributions from a REIT, the mix of a 6.13% headline yield, strong reported earnings growth and flagged cash flow constraints gives plenty of material for bearish arguments about how reliable those payouts may be over time.

With both risks and rewards in play, it is worth looking beyond the headlines and working through the details yourself before forming a view. To pressure test your thinking against the key positives and concerns already flagged by other investors, take a closer look at the 2 key rewards and 2 important warning signs.

See What Else Is Out There

data.title: Is Nu Holdings (NU) Fairly Priced After A 41% One-Year Share Price Gain
data.description:
• For investors wondering whether Nu Holdings is priced fairly or if the recent excitement has gone too far, this article walks through what the current market price might be implying about the stock.
  • Nu Holdings recently closed at US$15.10, with returns of 1.0% over 7 days, 8.3% over 30 days, an 11.3% decline year to date, and 41.0% over the last year, plus a 3-year return of 206.9%.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Nu Holdings as a major player in Latin American digital banking, with attention on how its expanding customer base and product suite might affect future profitability and risk. Commentators have also highlighted how its listing in the US gives global investors direct access to this regional banking story, which can influence trading interest and price swings.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Nu Holdings scores 2 out of 6 on perceived undervaluation, as shown by the valuation score. Next up is a closer look at the main valuation methods used for the stock and, at the end of the article, a different way to think about valuation that many investors overlook.

Nu Holdings scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Nu Holdings Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return required by shareholders, then capitalises those excess profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Nu Holdings, the model uses a Book Value of US$2.33 per share and a Stable EPS of US$1.37 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 15 analysts. The Average Return on Equity is 33.96%, compared with a Cost of Equity of US$0.45 per share. That gap produces an Excess Return of US$0.93 per share, which is what the model treats as value created beyond shareholders’ required return.

The Stable Book Value input is US$4.04 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 9 analysts. Feeding these figures into the Excess Returns framework gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$16.23 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$15.10, this implies the stock is around 6.9% undervalued, so the model suggests it is trading close to its calculated worth.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Approach 2: Nu Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Nu Holdings, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to current earnings, which many investors use as a shorthand for how the market is viewing the business today.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E generally reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher growth or perceived resilience can support a higher P/E, while higher risk or weaker profit quality usually points to a lower P/E.

Nu Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 25.6x. That sits above the Banks industry average of 11.9x and the peer group average of 14.8x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Nu Holdings is 24.4x. This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple industry or peer comparison because it blends factors such as earnings growth, risk profile, profit margin, industry, and market cap into a single number.

The actual P/E of 25.6x is only slightly higher than the Fair Ratio of 24.4x, which suggests the current price is broadly in line with what the model implies.

Approach 1: ASML Holding Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to the present.

For ASML Holding, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €8.37b. Analysts provide detailed forecasts for several years, with Simply Wall St extending these out further, and the projections used here reach a free cash flow figure of €20.34b in 2030.

After discounting these projected cash flows back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €728.48 per share. Compared with the current share price of €1,245.00, this implies that ASML trades at a 70.9% premium to this particular cash flow based estimate, so on this model the shares screen as expensive.

data.title: Is PepsiCo (PEP) Still Attractive After Recent Consumer Staples Inflation Headlines?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether PepsiCo at around US$156.99 is still a reasonable entry or more of a hold, the key question is how its current price lines up with the value of its future cash flows and assets.
  • The stock has returned 0.7% over the last 7 days, 4.6% over the last 30 days, 10.4% year to date, 15.2% over 1 year, a 6.4% decline over 3 years, and 27.9% over 5 years. This gives you a mix of shorter term momentum and mixed longer term performance to weigh.
  • Recent headlines around PepsiCo have focused on its role as a large consumer staples company, with attention on how its product portfolio and pricing power fit into broader discussions about inflation and consumer demand. This context often feeds into how investors think about the resilience and risk profile of the stock, which can influence shorter term market sentiment.
  • Right now PepsiCo scores a 3 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will break down what different valuation methods say about that number, before finishing with a more holistic way to think about whether the current price truly reflects the business.

PepsiCo delivered 15.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Beverage industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: PepsiCo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return.

For PepsiCo, the model starts with last twelve month free cash flow of about $7.9b and uses analyst forecasts and further extrapolations to project cash flows through 2035. For example, projected free cash flow in 2030 is $14.9b, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2027 at $11.1b and $11.8b. Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, where the first stage relies more on analyst estimates and the later years are extrapolated based on estimated growth rates.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back and added up, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $269.34 per share. The current share price is about $156.99, so this comparison implies the stock is roughly 41.7% undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests PepsiCo is undervalued by 41.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 63 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for PepsiCo.

Approach 2: PepsiCo Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For a profitable business like PepsiCo, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings the company is currently generating. It is a quick way to see how many dollars of price you are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk usually justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to align with a lower P/E.

PepsiCo currently trades at about 24.6x earnings. That is close to its Beverage peer average of 24.3x and above the broader Beverage industry average of 17.4x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 26.3x for PepsiCo, which reflects factors such as earnings growth expectations, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks in one figure.

This Fair Ratio can be more useful than a simple comparison to peers or the industry because it ties the P/E back to PepsiCo’s own characteristics rather than assuming all beverage companies deserve similar multiples. With the current P/E of 24.6x sitting below the Fair Ratio of 26.3x, this framework suggests the shares are undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your PepsiCo Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives take the story you believe about PepsiCo, including its future revenue, earnings and margins, turn that into a financial forecast, tie it to a Fair Value, and then compare that Fair Value to today’s price. All of this happens inside Simply Wall St’s Community page where millions of investors share views. For PepsiCo however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading PepsiCo Narratives:

🐂 PepsiCo Bull Case

Fair value: US$160.43

Implied discount to this fair value: about 2.1%

Revenue growth assumption: 2%

  • Focus on digitalization of the supply chain and data driven decision making across procurement, factories, transport and marketing.
  • Expansion in Vietnam, India and Africa, along with acquisitions such as Siete Foods, to broaden both geographic reach and product range.
  • Emphasis on environmental goals, stable margins and a belief that PepsiCo can offer more diversified and healthier products than some peers.

🐻 PepsiCo Bear Case

Fair value: US$116.35

Implied premium to this fair value: about 34.9%

Revenue growth assumption: 0%

  • Views PepsiCo as being at an inflection point, with revenue around US$91b and questions about whether new functional and health focused drinks can offset pressure on core snacks and sodas.
  • Highlights both tailwinds from prebiotic and functional beverages and headwinds from health concerns, affordability and strong competition, with an overall expectation of broadly flat revenue.
  • Sees dividends, cost control and supply chain efficiency as important supports for returns, even if top line growth stays limited.

These two narratives frame the current debate around PepsiCo, from a more optimistic view built around digital investment and international growth to a more cautious view that stresses flat revenues and income support.   • This move could lower practical barriers for small merchants in Australia, supporting higher seller participation and smoother logistics on eBay’s marketplace.
  • Next, we’ll explore how this enhanced in-store shipping convenience might influence eBay’s investment narrative around seller engagement and recommerce growth.

eBay Investment Narrative Recap

To own eBay you generally need to believe its recommerce focused marketplace can keep buyers and sellers engaged despite slower overall growth and strong competition. The new Australia Post label printing feature looks directionally helpful for seller engagement, but it does not materially change the biggest near term swing factors: how well focus categories hold up and whether logistics dependent buyer experience gaps narrow.

The most directly connected recent development is eBay’s expanded Klarna resale integration into six new markets, which, like the Australia Post partnership, reduces friction for casual and small sellers. Together, easier listing and easier shipping support the core catalyst of higher seller participation in recommerce, even as eBay continues to invest in AI tools, managed shipping and vertical experiences to deepen engagement across categories.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Some of the most optimistic analysts already expected eBay to reach about US$13.1 billion of revenue and US$2.5 billion of earnings by 2028, and saw managed shipping and sustainable recommerce as major tailwinds, whereas more cautious views worry that dependence on volatile focus categories could still cap the upside even if initiatives like the Australia Post partnership and broader C2C shipping efforts pay off.
